coredata.dc:title: Mechanisms behind multicolor tunable Near-Infrared triple emission in graphene quantum dots and ratio fluorescent probe for water detection

coredata.dc:description: Developing environmental-friendly and low-cost strategies of synthesizing red-emission graphene quantum dots (R-GQDs) is a considerable challenge. Herein, we present the green and facile preparation of R-GQDs by using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and catechol as precursors via oxidation/polymerization and Schiff base reaction at low temperature (80 °C) for 3 hrs. Results show that the prepared R-GQDs exhibit triple fluorescence emissions, which is enabled by the introduce of different nitrogen (pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and amino N) species on the surface of R-GQDs. Moreover, the R-GQDs are implemented to detect the moisture in different organic solvent. A highly sensitive ratiometric sensing of water in organic solvents is achieved, and the relationship between the change of fluorescence signal caused by moisture and the corresponding internal emission site is also determined. In the end, the multicolor light emissions of R-GQDs are realized by simply adjusting the polarity of surrounding solvents. And based on the solvatochromism of R-GQDs, the multicolor solid fluorescent powder and ink are prepared for illumination application. All in all, the above research work presents a novel R-GQDs for wide application in detecting and illumination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The progress in synthesis (o-PD and catechol react for 3 hrs at 80 °C, the yield is 90%), properties (the triple-emission from nitrogen-related groups, solvatochromism), and applications (water sensing, multicolor powder, and ink) of the prepared R-GQDs.

(a) TEM image of R-GQDs (Inset shows the HR-TEM and FFT images). (b) Particle size distribution measured by TEM. (c) XRD pattern of the R-GQDs. (d) Raman spectrum of the R-GQDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of catechol, o-PD, and R-GQDs. (f) XPS spectrum of R-GQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (g) C 1 s, (h) N 1 s and (i) O 1 s.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of R-GQDs (Inset: the optical images of R-GQDs solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right)). (b) Emission spectra of the R-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths (460–640 nm). (c) Excitation-emission matrix graph map for R-GQDs. (d) Summary of emission wavelength and quantum yield of GQDs obtained in this work and previous reports. (e) The stability of Em-1, Em-2, and Em-3 of the R-GQDs incubated at ambient conditions for 24 days. (f) Photostability of the R-GQDs under the 365 nm xenon lamp for 12 hrs.

DFT calculation results (molecular structure, LUMO, HOMO, and energy gap) of R-GQDs with (a) different amount of graphitic N, (b) different amount of CO, and (c) different forms of nitrogen (pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and amino N).

(a) Normalized PL emission spectra of the R-GQDs dispersed in various solvents under the optimal excitation wavelength. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the R-GQDs in different solvents. (c) Emission wavelength as a function of the solvent polarity parameter, ET N; the solid line is a linear fit of the data. (d) Schematic illustration of the possible energy level diagram and multicolor emission processes of R-GQDs in various solvents.

PL spectra of R-GQDs in different solvents: (a) ethanol, (c) acetone, (e) DMF with different water content. Linear graphs of F1/F1,0, F2/F2,0, F3/F3,0, and F1/F2 versus concentration of water in different solvents (b) ethanol, (d) acetone, (f) DMF.

(a) Photographs of R-GQDs powder based on different solvents (ethyl acetate, DMSO, and ethanol) under UV light. (b) CIE coordinates of the three R-GQDs powder. (c) Photographs of printing patterns on rice paper with R-GQDs ink in different solvents (ethyl acetate, DMSO, and ethanol) under UV light. Images of hand-written using the R-GQDs-based fluorescence ink on rice paper under UV light irradiation: (d) English poetry and (e) Chinese poetry.

head.ce:title: Mechanisms behind multicolor tunable Near-Infrared triple emission in graphene quantum dots and ratio fluorescent probe for water detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing environmental-friendly and low-cost strategies of synthesizing red-emission graphene quantum dots (R-GQDs) is a considerable challenge. Herein, we present the green and facile preparation of R-GQDs by using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and catechol as precursors via oxidation/polymerization and Schiff base reaction at low temperature (80 °C) for 3 hrs. Results show that the prepared R-GQDs exhibit triple fluorescence emissions, which is enabled by the introduce of different nitrogen (pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and amino N) species on the surface of R-GQDs. Moreover, the R-GQDs are implemented to detect the moisture in different organic solvent. A highly sensitive ratiometric sensing of water in organic solvents is achieved, and the relationship between the change of fluorescence signal caused by moisture and the corresponding internal emission site is also determined. In the end, the multicolor light emissions of R-GQDs are realized by simply adjusting the polarity of surrounding solvents. And based on the solvatochromism of R-GQDs, the multicolor solid fluorescent powder and ink are prepared for illumination application. All in all, the above research work presents a novel R-GQDs for wide application in detecting and illumination.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the photoluminescent (PL) of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted researches’ tremendous attention because of their controllable response (i.e., light emissions) toward environmental stimulations. Such property endows them as a promising candidate for sensing and lighting. Moreover, these GQDs also show ultra-stable PL performance and good biocompatibility, further expanding their potential applications as bio-labels and drug carriers.[1–4] Presently, the design and synthesizing of various GQDs with multi-color features is based on the established understanding of the correlation between the PL property and GQD structure.[5–7] And among those prepared GQDs, the red emissive GQDs (R-GQDs) demonstrate strong penetrability to the organism and good bioimaging capability, which puts them in the most preferable position for biological and medical applications.[8–10] Up to date, dramatic progress has been achieved on R-GQDs studies, however, the wide application of R-GQDs is still hindered by different factors, such as inefficient synthesis routes, low quantum yield (QY), and weak luminescence intensity.[11,12] Therefore, an in-depth understanding of the PL mechanism is essentially needed to guide the modulation of the GQDs property and achieve better performance. Meanwhile, developing energy-efficient synthesis strategies to produce R-GQDs is of great practical significance.

Generally, the PL process mainly comes from the aromatic domain of the GQDs. Any environmental variation will polarize the electronic structure of these domains and consequently alter the PL emission, leading to a detectable fluorescence signal change. For instance, fluorescence enhancement stimulated by the hydrogen-bonding interactions in aqueous solution or fluorescence quenching originated from the aggregation of the aromatic structures have been reported, indicating the good capability of the GQDs in detecting the moisture in organic solvents, pharmaceutical products, food, and chemicals.[13,14] Relying on the above mechanism, most of the fluorescence analyses focus on one-peak intensity change, such as fluorescence quenching or enhancement that stimulated by external perturbation.[15,16] However, compared with the single-emission GQDs, the triple-emission GQDs present more intrinsic peaks, which processes the advantage of further enhancing the identity and avoiding being influenced by the systematical and environmental aspects. In addition, systematic studies on the PL mechanism demonstrate the aromatic domain of the GQDs can be tuned by different functional groups, which results in the split of the energy levels that controls the light absorption and emission.[15–17] Among these different modulation schemes, the nitrogen-containing groups are extremely flexible in tuning the luminescence properties of GQDs as nitrogen can form a variety of surface states, such as pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, amino N, and so on.[18–20] Therefore, good combinations of nitrogen-containing forms is the key to achieving the targeting fluorescence signals with external quenching agents.[21]

Herein, we employed the R-GQDs as a prototypical system, and the impact on the PL property of the attached nitrogen-containing groups on the R-GQD is investigated. Experimentally, different R-GQDs have been synthesized through spontaneous oxidation/polymerization and Schiff reactions. The obtained R-GQDs show three independent, bright red emission peaks at 599 nm, 640 nm, and 710 nm, respectively. The theoretical simulations evidence that these peaks are related to the excitation/emission of different nitrogen types (pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and amino N) on the surface of R-GQDs. Thereafter, the R-GQDs are used to perform a fast fluorescence detection of the water in organic solvents. The fluorescence intensities derived from these three nitrogen-containing optical centers show different response behavior. Inspired by this discovery, a ratiometric fluorescent platform with broad linear detection range from 0 to 40% and low detection limits (e.g., 0.041% in ethanol, 0.067% in acetone, 0.078% in DMF) is constructed. Meanwhile, the multicolor solid fluorescent powder and ink are prepared for illumination application. Thus, this work not only provides a simple, scalable, and energy-efficient synthesis method to synthesize R-GQDs at low temperature but also expands the application of R-GQDs in lighting, display, information storage, etc.

O-phenylenediamine (o-PD) (99.5%), p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) (≥99.0%), m-phenylenediamine (m-PD) (99.5%), m-dihydroxybenzene (99%), catechol (≥99.5%), formamide (≥99%), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (≥99.9%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (≥99.9%), acetone (≥99.7%), ethanol (≥99.8%), ethyl acetate (≥99.9%), toluene (≥99.5%), NaOH (≥96.0%), H2SO4 (≥96.0%), Rhodamine B (>99.0%), polyethylene glycol (PEG) (average Mn 6000) and other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and utilized as obtained without further purification. Ultrapure water used throughout all experiments is purified via a Millipore system with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm.

The morphology and size distribution of the R-GQDs was measured by using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Model JEM-2100F, 200 kV, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Ltd.). Raman spectrum (Thermo Fisher Dxi) was obtained by using an Ar+ laser with a wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi) was employed to determine the surface chemical composition and functional groups. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a Nexus 470 (Thermo Fisher) spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured by X-ray diffraction (PANalytical X’Pert Pro MPD). The 1H Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectrum was obtained on a Bruker AV-400 MHz spectrometer. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, the UV–vis absorption spectra, and the quantum yield were measured with a multifunction fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS55) from PerkinElmer Instruments (U.K.) at room temperature. The digital photographs were taken with iPhone 12 (Apple Inc.). All the simulations were performed at the B3LYP level [22,23] of functional with the at 6-311G++(d,p) basis set [24,25] using the Gaussian 09 software.[26] The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) orbitals were plotted by using the Gaussview package.[27]

The reaction scheme for the preparation, properties, and applications of R-GQDs was presented in Fig. 1 . 0.11 g (1 mmol) of catechol, 0.18 mg (1 mmol) of o-phenylenediamine, and 20.0 mL ethanol were mixed thoroughly and heated at 80 °C for 3 hrs in a thermostat water bath. After cooling to room temperature, the crude products were filtered with a 0.22 μm filtration membrane and dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for 24 hrs to remove the unreacted substances. Finally, the pure product were dried in vacuum for 12 hrs, and then stored at 4 °C before use.

Rhodamine B dissolved in absolute ethanol (refractive index η = 1.096, 25 °C) was used as a reference. To minimize self-absorption effect, the absorbance of the reference solution and the suspensions of GQDs at the optimum excitation wavelength were controlled below 0.1 by diluting the stock solution. Then, the relative QY can be calculated through the following equation:[28]

where I represents the integrated fluorescence intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A refers to the absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength of 535 nm. The subscript ‘‘st” and ‘‘x” refers to Rhodamine B and R-GQDs, respectively. For all these solutions, ηx/ηst is approximately equal to 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: First, DMF, ethanol, and acetone with different concentrations (0 to 100%) of water were prepared. Then, 0.1 mg R-GQDs were directly dispersed into the as-prepared solvents (20 mL) and sonicated thoroughly for 15 min by ultrasonication. The optimum emission fluorescence spectra of the solution were recorded at room temperature.

The R-GQDs are prepared using catechol and o-PD as precursors at low temperature (80 °C) without special equipment and oxidation reagents. The synthesis process of R-GQDs is shown in Figure S1 . First of all, the two active phenolic hydroxyl groups of catechol are oxidized, forming o-benzoquinone.[29] Meanwhile, as the –NH2 groups of o-PD are highly functional, thus o-PD dimer, o-PD polymer, and 2, 3-dinitro-phenazinemethane are produced through the oxidation/polymerization reactions of o-PD.[30] Then, Schiff reaction occurs between the –NH2 group of o-PD/o-PD oxidation products and the CO of o-benzoquinone. After that, various polymer fragments are formed via dehydration, condensation, and polymerization.[31] These polymers are further carbonized and aromatized to aromatic clusters under low temperature and eventually transformed into GQDs.

The QY and performance of R-GQDs is determined by the reaction conditions. Therefore, the effects of the precursors, feed ratio, reaction temperature, and reaction time have been investigated in detail. For aniline structures, the o-PD is more active than p-PD and m-PD when they react with catechol. And it shows in Figure S2
(a) that, when the p-PD or m-PD reacts with catechol under the same condition, the former tends to produce yellow light emission, but only blue emission is observed in the latter. This is because that the o-PD with an ortho-position functional group is more likely to combine with catechol. As a result, it induces more chromophores into the conjugate structures of GQDs, featuring as a red-shifted emission.[32,33] Furthermore, when the feed ratio of the o-PD and catechol decreases from 3:1 to 1:1, the emission color of the product varies from green to red ( Figure S2
(b)), indicating that the increasing oxidization level causes the redshift of the emission wavelength of GQDs.[28] Additionally, to estimate the role of ethanol in the synthesis process, formamide and water have also been used as solvothermal media ( Figure S2
(c)). Compared with the strong red triple emission in ethanol, a green emission in formamide and even nearly non-luminescent in water are observed in the final products, which suggests that the ethanol acts as a nucleophile and affects the final GQDs.[33] Finally, the reaction temperature and time results on the fluorescence intensity of R-GQDs are observed in Figure S2
(d).

Fig. 2
(a) shows a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the as-obtained R-GQDs, revealing that these R-GQDs are well monodispersed in ethanol. The high-resolution TEM image presented in the inset displays a typical well-resolved crystal lattice with a spacing of 0.24 nm, representing the (100) in-plane lattice fringe of graphene.[34] The size distribution analysis of R-GQDs is shown in Fig. 2 (b). It shows that the synthesized R-GQDs mainly range from 3 to 8.5 nm with an average size of 5.7 nm in the selected area. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the R-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2 (c), shows an apparent peak at around 21.4° that is contributed by the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials.[35] While the Raman spectrum of R-GQDs in Fig. 2 (d) reveals two peaks at 1344 cm−1 (D band) and 1598 cm−1 (G band), corresponding to areas of disorder or defects and sp2 carbon networks in the carbon materials, respectively.[36] The calculated intensity ratio ID/IG is 0.72, indicating a high degree of graphitization, which is consistent with the TEM results.

The FT-IR and XPS were also used to study the functional groups and terminal elements of R-GQDs. The FT-IR spectra comparison of catechol (blue line), o-PD (green line), and R-GQDs (red line) are shown in Fig. 2 (e). For catechol and o-PD, the stretching vibration peaks of OH/NH, CC, and CO are above 3359–3600 cm−1, 1503 cm−1, and 1100 cm−1.[37–39] For R-GQDs, these peaks are also observed, which indicates that R-GQDs inherit the natural properties of raw materials during the synthesis process. In addition, sharp peaks associated with amide linkage (–CONH-) at 1650 cm−1 and 1556 cm−1 prove the dehydration condensation of catechol and o-PD during the formation of R-GQDs.[40] Noteworthy, the R-GQDs show the stronger absorption bands from 3200 to 3500 cm−1 and 1000–1200 cm−1, indicating that there are much more nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of R-GQDs than that of catechol and o-PD.[41] Furthermore, a new and strong stretching vibration between 2919 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 is observed, which is attributed to stable polyaromatic hydrocarbon structures with high energy bonds (CH), and this peak also confirms the successful synthesis of R-GQDs.[42] The full XPS spectrum of the R-GQDs (Fig. 2 (f)) presents three typical peaks centered at 285.1 eV, 399.4 eV, and 532.3 eV, indicating the existence of C (79.66%), N (15.78%), and O (4.56%) elements in the R-GQDs. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 (g)) consists of four peaks located at 284.2 eV, 285.4 eV, 285.9 eV, and 289.5 eV, which can be assigned to CC/CC, CN, CO, and CN/CO, respectively.[43,44] The N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 (h)) can be fitted into four peaks at 398.3 eV, 399.0 eV, 399.6 eV, and 400.1 eV, indexing to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, amino N, and graphitic N.[45,46] The O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 (i)) contains two peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.5 eV, which belong to CO and CO.[47] These results from XPS are in good accordance with those of FT-IR analysis.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of R-GQDs are measured to study their optical properties. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-GQDs demonstrates multiple electronic absorption transitions in R-GQDs (Fig. 3
(a), black line). The two absorption bands at 241 nm and 285 nm can be ascribed to the intrinsic state (π-π*) transition of aromatic sp2 domains (CC) and the defect states (n-π*) transition of CO/CO bonds, respectively.[48] A wide absorption band from 450 nm to 600 nm is observed in the visible region, which can be assigned to the complicated surface states of R-GQDs.[49] Besides, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of R-GQDs (Fig. 3 (a), green line) overlaps well with the absorption spectrum in the visible region, showing that nitrogen-related surface structures and states are responsible for the red triple emission.[50] The fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 3 (a), red line) demonstrates that the triple emission of R-GQDs occurs at 599 nm, 640 nm, and 710 nm (designated as Em-1, Em-2, and Em-3, respectively) under optimal excitation wavelength (535 nm). The R-GQDs solution is brownish red under the sunlight and exhibits strong red fluorescence under irradiation with UV light in the inset of Fig. 3 (a). Furthermore, the emission (λem) of R-GQDs is almost independent of excitation when the excitation (λex) moves from 460 nm to 640 nm (Fig. 3 (b)), which is attributed to the relatively uniform size distribution and surface states of the R-GQDs.[51] Fig. 3 (c) reveals a more intuitive view of the relationship between emission and excitation of R-GQDs by the two-dimensional mapping of λem and λex, which shows three emission peak positions of 599 nm, 640 nm, and 710 nm under the excitation of 535 nm, respectively. Most GQDs reported in previous studies emit blue or green light, and the few GQDs talking in the red region are often limited by low QYs (Fig. 3 (d)).[52] Compared with previous reports, the prepared R-GQDs have a higher quantum yield (35%) with yielded (90%) (Table S1 and Table S2). For practical applications based on fluorescent probes, the fluorescence stabilities of R-GQDs at long-term storage, photobleaching time, and ionic strength (based on NaCl concentration) are also very important. And as shown in Fig. 3 (e-f), the fluorescence intensity of R-GQDs decreased slightly after storing at room temperature for 24 days or ultraviolet exposure for 12 hrs, which indicates that the R-GQDs have good optical stability and photobleaching resistance. Furthermore, the prepared R-GQDs show relatively stable PL intensity in NaCl solutions with different concentrations in the range of 0–100 mM ( Figure S3 ). The above results demonstrate that R-GQDs are promising for fluorescent probe detection due to their good optical and salt stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence mechanism of red triple-emission GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Many researchers have been devoted to studying the underlying mechanism for the effective red emission of R-GQDs with three emission centers.[53–55] Considering the results of R-GQDs from the structural and optical characterizations, the possible PL mechanism is investigated. To identify the emission origin of R-GQDs, we perform dialysis of the R-GQDs in ethanol, and the PL map of dialyzed R-GQDs at different times is compared. As shown in Figure S4 , the PL maps of dialyzed R-GQDs are similar to each other for all times. It implies that the emissive origin of R-GQDs cannot be isolated from the R-GQDs through dialysis. Thus the red emission does not originate from fluorescent molecules but rather surface states. Therefore, we speculate that the effective red emission of R-GQDs is mainly attributed to the great content of surface oxidation and graphitic N, which has been reported in the previous work.[28,29] For example, Bao et al. proved that the surface states are the key factor in tuning the emission of GQDs, and that a gradual increase in oxidation degree could lead to red-shifted emission.[56] Nguyen et al. noted that GQDs could be oxidized to produce smaller spatial local defects, leading to red emission.[57] Hola et al. caused a fluorescence shift from blue to red in GQDs by increasing the graphitic nitrogen content and explaining graphitic nitrogen's role in red-shifted fluorescence.[39] The n-electron cloud of oxygen-containing functional groups (such as CO, –OH) and graphitic N structural configuration can create new energy levels in R-GQDs, which reduces the energy of electronic transition and realizes the redshift of the wavelength. To prove this theory, density functional theory (DFT) calculations are carried out on the dimensional model of R-GQDs with different graphitic nitrogen and CO contents to simulate the orbital energy levels and bandgaps. As shown in Fig. 4
(a-b) and Figure S5 , with the gradual increase of graphitic nitrogen and CO content, the bandgap of the molecule decreased from 4.7 eV to 2.33 eV and 1.55 eV, respectively, indicating that the increase of graphitic nitrogen and surface oxygen-containing functional groups lead to the narrowing of the bandgap and redshift of the fluorescence wavelength. The above theoretical evidence is consistent with the experimental results. Meanwhile, graphitic N is considered as an important factor affecting the fluorescence quantum yield of R-GQDs. Since the graphitic N exists in the sp2 domain of the carbon core, its two non-bonding electrons can increase the electron cloud density of the entire conjugated π-domain while reducing the non-radiative recombination center of the R-GQDs, thereby increasing the QY.[58]

In addition, the unique triple-peak emission of R-GQDs is also investigated. During the synthesis process of R-GQDs, the surface amino groups of o-PD will produce different groups (pyridinic N, pyrrolic N) or remain intact (amino N) on the surface R-GQDs. Since there is a strong relationship between the structural features and PL properties of GQDs, the multiple emission states on the surface of R-GQDs may be linked to the different nitrogen-containing functional groups. As shown in Figure S6 , the 1H NMR spectrum exhibits obvious signals in the range of 6.3–8.9 ppm for R-GQDs, which can be attributed to pyrrolic N and pyridinic N. The signal at 2.44 ppm is indicative of the amino N.[59,60] Hence, combined with XPS (Fig. 3 (f-i)), FT-IR (Fig. 3 (e)), and NMR results, it can be found that these nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of R-GQDs play an irreplaceable role in the luminescence process, which dominate the tripe-peak emission of R-GQDs. This PL shift due to structural change among three types of R-GQDs emission is consistent with other reported studies.[18,20,39] In addition, we referred to the triple emission of R-GQDs (599 nm, 640 nm, and 710 nm) are derived from pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and amino N, respectively. To verify this, we recorded PL maps for R-GQDs under acidic conditions with different pH because nitrogen-containing forms of emission centers may have distinct response towards acidic environments.[45] As shown in Figure S7
(a-c), compared to the neutral condition, the three emission centers of R-GQDs are weakened under acidic conditions. This is consistent with previous reports that nitrogen-related emission centers decrease with increasing acidity.[18] To carefully analyze the pH sensitivity of the three emission centers, the relative PL intensities of the three emission centers at different pH conditions are recorded in Figure S7
(d). The PL intensity of the three emissions is significantly reduced at pH = 5 to 38.3%, 41.7%, and 45.5% for Em-1, Em-2, and Em-3, respectively. At pH = 3, the fluorescence intensity of Em-1, Em-2, and Em-3 continue to decrease to 21.4%, 29%, and 38.6%. Meanwhile, the slight redshift of the PL band is observed for Em-1, Em-2, and Em-3, and the degree of displacement decreases from Em-1 to Em-3 ( Figure S7
(e)). Generally, it is known that these quenching and redshift of emissions at acidic conditions are originated from the protonation of surface functional groups. In particular, the addition of hydrogen changes the surface charge of R-GQDs and their energy structure. Consequently, the protonated functional groups will act as a non-radiative channel, resulting in fluorescence quenching under acidic conditions.[61] However, the protonation ability of N-related functional groups is influenced by its basic properties.[62] And the dissociation constant (pKa) values of pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and amino N are 23, 5.2, and 4.6, which exhibit the basic strength of functional groups.[63,64] Therefore, the N-related functional groups with high pKa exhibit a more sensitive response to pH, resulting in greater attenuation and PL redshift. As a result, pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and amino N may serve as the source of the triple emission of R-GQDs, resulting in different PL intensity attenuations in response to pH values, which is also consistent with our inference.

Other than that, we also perform the density functional theory calculations to elucidate the effect of different kinds of nitrogen on R-GQDs emission. The molecular models (including two pyridinic N, two pyrrolic N or two amine groups) are selected to study the orbital energy levels and bandgaps. As shown in Fig. 4 (c), with the introduction of pyrrolic N, pyridinic N and amino N, the band gap of R-GQDs is decreased gradually, compared to pure condition. And the band gap of pyrrolic N, pyridinic N and amino N is −4.61 eV, −4.44 eV and −4.29 eV, respectively. Therefore, the DFT analysis results demonstrated that the emissive centers at 599 nm and 640 nm are originated from pyrrolic N and pyridinic N structures, and the 710 nm emission peak is derived from amino N structure, which are consistent with the structural investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of R-GQDs to solvent for tunable light emission
ce:section.ce:para:
After dissolving R-GQDs in different solvents, it is found that R-GQDs have good solubility in different solvents, including toluene, ethyl acetate, acetone, ethanol, DMSO, DMF, formamide, water and so on. With the increment of solvent polarity, the maximum fluorescence emission peaks of R-GQDs present a redshift from 542 nm to 622 nm under a single excitation wavelength, thus resulting in a fluorescence color change from green to red (Fig. 5
(a)). The corresponding fluorescence photos under UV lamp are shown in Figure S8 . The representative two-dimensional contour maps of emission and excitation in Figure S9 give more details to reflect the redshifts of the emission centers with the increased polarity. The corresponding emission wavelengths of the three peak positions are shown in Table S3. The emission shift of Em-1 is 80 nm, the Em-2 exhibits a shift of 72 nm, and the Em-3 shifts by 61 nm. It is mainly attribute to the stronger induction of the aromatic conjugated structure originated from the solvent polarity.

To explore the solvent-dependent properties of R-GQDs, UV–vis spectra are characterized. As shown in Fig. 5 (b), the increment of the solvent polarity induces a redshift of the absorption wavelength, indicating that the solvent's polarizations can modulate the energy gaps of the R-GQDs.[51] The calculated energy gaps are presented in Figure S10 . As the polarity increases, the energy gap gradually declines (except for ethanol), which is consistent with the observed redshift in PL emission. The relative solvent polarity parameter (ET N) can describe this discoloration of R-GQDs with solvent.[65] Fig. 5 (c) exhibits the relationship between the optimal PL emission of the three peak positions and the relative solvent polarity parameter with good linearity. The anomaly in ethanol is due to the strong hydrogen bonds between oxygen and nitrogen atoms on the surface of R-GQDs and alcohol molecules.[66]

The above results show a visual illustration of possible energy level diagrams and emission processes of the R-GQDs in various solvents in Fig. 5 (d). Generally, the redshift of PL emission in a high polar solvent can be induced by edge N structures and a strong solvation effect.[19,67] First, edge N structures, such as pyrrolic and pyridinic forms, can increase the charge-carrier density in a high polar solvent through the interaction with the surrounding solvents.[68] Second, when the electron is excited from the ground state, it can be stabilized by changing the dipole direction of the surrounding solvent molecules. As solvent polarity increases, the energy required to change the dipole direction of the solvent molecules also increases because of the strong solvent dipole moment. As a result, exciting electrons in a high polar solvent lose more energy than that in the low polar solvent, resulting in a redshift of the PL emission.[16,69]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para:
Encouraged by the unique optical properties and novel organic-soluble characteristics, we explore the feasibility of R-GQDs for the quantitative determination of trace water in organic solvents. This interesting feature makes R-GQDs a potential candidate for ratiometric fluorescence detection. Therefore, various organic solvents which are miscible with water, such as acetone, ethanol, and DMF, are tested using our R-GQDs as fluorescence probes. Taking the ethanol as the model solvent, we dissolve the prepared R-GQDs powders in ethanol solution with different water contents (0% to 100%). Specifically, the fluorescence emission intensity of R-GQDs at F1, F2, and F3 (F1, F2, and F3 are defined as the intensities of the three emission peaks centered at 599 nm, 640 nm, and 710 nm with different water content) all decrease continuously as the water content increasing from 0% to 100% (Fig. 6
(a)). The corresponding fluorescence image under the UV lamp is shown in Figure S11 . At first, the intensity of F1, F2, and F3 are studied. As shown in Fig. 6 (b), the ratio of PL intensity at F1 and F2 (F1/F2) decreases linearly with the water concentration increasing from 0% to 40%. The correlation coefficient R2 = 0.998 proves excellent correlation. The detection limit is determined to be 0.041%. The other ratios of PL intensity at F1/F1,0, F2/F2,0, and F3/F3,0 (F1,0, F2,0, and F3,0 are defined as the intensities of the three emission peaks centered at 599 nm, 640 nm, and 710 nm without water) also exhibit linear relationship. Still, the corresponding R2 of 0.996, 0.995, and 0.991 are smaller than that of F1/F2. The corresponding detection limit (Table S4) is higher than F1/F2, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence detection with dual referential peaks system is superior to single peak fluorescence detection.

Besides ethanol, we also utilize R-GQDs as a fluorescence probe to detect the trace water in acetone and DMF. The fluorescence response data for detecting water content in acetone and DMF is shown in Fig. 6 (c-f). As the water concentration increases from 0 to 40%, F1/F1,0, F2/F2,0, F3/F3,0 and F1/F2 decreases linearly, and the PL intensity ratio of F1/F2 in acetone and DMF gives a low detection limit of 0.067% and 0.078%, respectively. The corresponding detailed PL variation of F1/F1,0, F2/F2,0, and F3/F3,0 with water content from 0 to 100% is shown in Figure S12 . Meanwhile, as shown in Figure S13 , the detail intensity variation of F1/F2, F2/F3 and F1/F3 in different solvents are also investigated. A linear relationship is established for water content in the range of 0–40%, and the corresponding correlation coefficients and detection limits are summarized in Table S4. Compared with F2/F3 and F1/F3 in acetone and DMF, the F1/F2 in ethanol shows a lower detection limit. In addition, Table S5 summarizes current studies about utilizing GQDs-based fluorescence materials for detecting trace water, and the R-GQDs-based sensing platform in this work has a much higher sensitivity and wider detection range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor fluorescent powder and ink based on the solvent effect of R-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the solvatochromic phenomenon of R-GQDs, we have succeeded in achieving a tunable solid-state fluorescence GQDs powder by simply adjusting the solvent for the first time, which will meet more practical applications such as lighting and displays. The specific preparation process of polychromatic PEG powder is shown in Figure S14 . As shown in Fig. 7
(a), the GQDs-PEG powder exhibits green, yellow, red colors, respectively. The corresponding Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates are recorded in Fig. 7 (b). As we all know, the GQDs in the solid state generally have self-quenching properties due to the aggregation effect.[70] The PEG polymer chains are the key to achieving solid-state fluorescence. The R-GQDs and solvent molecules are encapsulated due to the protection by the polymer chain meshwork.[71] The GQDs cannot directly attach and aggregate even in the solid-state. Therefore, we have successfully obtained multicolor solid-state fluorescent powder.

R-GQDs with different solvents (including ethyl acetate, DMSO and ethanol) can also be used as fluorescent inks with varying emissions of fluorescence from green to red. Firstly, R-GQDs with different solvents are injected into the stamp to print animal patterns and the Ningbo University logo. As shown in Fig. 7 (c), the animal patterns and Ningbo University logo exhibits different color (green, yellow, and red) on the rice paper under UV light irradiation. Subsequently, these solutions are used as ink to write fluorescent characters on rice paper by a brush. As shown in Fig. 7 (d-e), benefiting from the unique solvatochromism behavior of R-GQDs, an English poem and a masterpiece “Viewing the Waterfall at Mount Lu” by Li Bai (a great poet in the Tang Dynasty, China) show bright green, yellow and red fluorescence under UV light.

In conclusion, the research and development of multicolor graphene quantum dots and luminescence mechanism have become the focus of scientific researchers. In recent works, researchers used o-PD with other materials as precursors to prepare nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots.[9,19,48] But there are still disadvantages, such as complicated preparation process, low yield and unclear luminescence mechanism. Therefore, in this work, R-GQDs with tunable solvent discoloration behavior are successfully prepared via low temperature (80 °C) Schiff reaction using o-PD and catechol as precursors. The prepared R-GQDs show unique triple emission peaks and the red fluorescence is mainly attributed to the abundant content of surface oxidation and graphitic N on the surface of R-GQDs. The underlying mechanism of the triple-fluorescence phenomenon is investigated through extensive studies using theoretical analysis, simulation, and other characterization. The results suggest three emissive states: pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and amino N, while the graphitic N enhances the QY of R-GQDs (35%). Meanwhile, the three peak positions show obvious solvent-dependent light emissions and different fluorescence quenching responses to water in organic solvents. A ratiometric fluorescent probe for water sensing is developed based on the R-GQDs. Besides, R-GQDs are further exploited as colorful solid-state fluorescent powder and fluorescent inks. Overall, this work provides new information into the preparation, luminescence mechanism, and multicolor tuning of R-GQDs. It constitutes an important reference for the future application of GQDs in optical molecular sensors, illumination, displays, information security, and others.
